howItWorks.intro: This cycling power calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Watts per Kg = Power ÷ (Rider Weight in kg)
Climbing Speed ≈ Power ÷ (Weight × g × Gradient + Rolling Resistance)
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What is a good watts per kg for cycling?
faq.answer: Beginner: 1.5-2.5 W/kg. Recreational: 2.5-3.2. Club racer: 3.2-4.0. Competitive amateur: 4.0-5.0. Professional: 5.5-7.0 W/kg. World Tour winners sustain 6.0-6.5 W/kg on major climbs.

faq.question: What is FTP in cycling?
faq.answer: Functional Threshold Power (FTP) is the highest average power you can sustain for approximately one hour. It's the gold standard for measuring cycling fitness and setting training zones.

faq.question: How does weight affect climbing speed?
faq.answer: On steep climbs, watts per kilogram determines speed. Losing 1 kg at the same power is equivalent to gaining about 3-5 watts on a 7% climb. This is why climbers tend to be lighter.

faq.question: Is the Cycling Power Output Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard sports analytics formulas trusted by athletes, coaches, and fans. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

sections.title: What Is the Cycling Power Output Calculator?
sections.body: The Cycling Power Output Calculator is a free online tool designed for athletes, coaches, and fans who need quick, accurate calculations in the sports analytics space. By entering your rider + bike weight, ftp, road gradient, you get instant results including power output, watts per kilogram, climbing speed. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting power output right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In sports analytics, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by athletes, coaches, and fans worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to analyze performance with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your rider + bike weight and need to find the right power output. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Rider + Bike Weight (lbs)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your rider + bike weight — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: ftp, road gradient, effort level. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your power output, watts per kilogram, climbing speed. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your rider + bike weight before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both power output and watts per kilogram — they work together to give you the full picture.

If you're unsure about your effort level, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
